title: Expecting Better
author: Emily Oster
subline: Why the conventional pregnancy wisdom is wrong — and what you really need to know.
customHTML:
<svg viewBox="0 0 1600 2400" preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid slice" style="position:absolute;inset:0;width:100%;height:100%">
  <!-- A bell curve (the 'normal distribution' of the data) drawn in hairlines in the bottom 2/3, with the confidence interval shaded RED. Title sits above. -->
  <g transform="translate(800 1640)" fill="none">
    <!-- Axes -->
    <line x1="-700" y1="320" x2="700" y2="320" stroke="#4c4c4c" stroke-width="1"/>
    <line x1="0" y1="-280" x2="0" y2="320" stroke="#4c4c4c" stroke-width="1"/>
    <!-- The bell curve (two stacked sigmoids) -->
    <path d="M -700 320 C -500 320 -480 -270 0 -280 C 480 -270 500 320 700 320" stroke="#cccccc" stroke-width="2"/>
    <!-- Filled-in region under the curve -->
    <path d="M -700 320 C -500 320 -480 -270 0 -280 C 480 -270 500 320 700 320 L 700 320 L -700 320 Z" fill="#cccccc" opacity="0.05"/>
    <!-- The 95% confidence interval (z = ±1.96 std devs) — drawn as a red shaded band centered on the peak, with dashed boundary lines -->
    <g>
      <!-- Left boundary line -->
      <line x1="-260" y1="-280" x2="-260" y2="320" stroke="#fc1c46" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/>
      <!-- Right boundary line -->
      <line x1="260" y1="-280" x2="260" y2="320" stroke="#fc1c46" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/>
      <!-- Filled CI band -->
      <path d="M -260 320 L -260 0 C -260 -260 -160 -278 0 -280 C 160 -278 260 -260 260 0 L 260 320 Z" fill="#fc1c46" opacity="0.12"/>
    </g>
    <!-- The MEAN marker -->
    <line x1="0" y1="-280" x2="0" y2="320" stroke="#fc1c46" stroke-width="2.5"/>
    <circle cx="0" cy="-280" r="10" fill="#fc1c46"/>
    <!-- Tiny tick marks at -3σ, -2σ, -1σ, mean, +1σ, +2σ, +3σ -->
    <g stroke="#4c4c4c" stroke-width="1">
      <line x1="-540" y1="320" x2="-540" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="-260" y1="320" x2="-260" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="-130" y1="320" x2="-130" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="0" y1="320" x2="0" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="130" y1="320" x2="130" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="260" y1="320" x2="260" y2="332"/>
      <line x1="540" y1="320" x2="540" y2="332"/>
    </g>
    <!-- Labels under ticks -->
    <g font-family="'Sui','Söhne','Inter',system-ui,sans-serif" font-size="11" font-weight="500" fill="#4c4c4c" text-anchor="middle" letter-spacing="0.04em">
      <text x="-540" y="350">-3σ</text>
      <text x="-260" y="350" fill="#fc1c46">-2σ</text>
      <text x="-130" y="350">-1σ</text>
      <text x="0" y="350" fill="#fc1c46">μ</text>
      <text x="130" y="350">+1σ</text>
      <text x="260" y="350" fill="#fc1c46">+2σ</text>
      <text x="540" y="350">+3σ</text>
    </g>
  </g>
</svg>
rationale: Oster is an economist who treated pregnancy as a data problem. The cover IS a normal distribution bell curve, with axes, tick marks at -3σ to +3σ, and a RED 95% confidence interval band. The mean is the sole red vertical line, with a red dot at the peak. Title sits at the top, weight 500 command, mixed-case. Brand 'DATA' in the bottom-right; pill 'p<0.05' at the bottom — the threshold of statistical significance that re-frames the entire book.
